title: Eradicating Wolbachia: A tetracycline-based approach for Drosophila melanogaster and other fruit flies
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"2t5a3","text":"Wolbachia, a maternally inherited endosymbiont, can significantly influence the biology of its arthropod hosts, including Drosophila melanogaster. Its presence can confound experimental results. To overcome this challenge, we present a protocol for eliminating Wolbachia from Drosophila melanogaster and other fruit fly species using tetracycline antibiotics. By treating flies with tetracycline during development, we successfully eradicate Wolbachia from the host lineage, enabling precise and controlled experiments. This protocol provides a valuable tool for the Drosophila research community. Wolbachia researchers can also benefit from this protocol to compare Wolbachia-bearing and Wolbachia-free insects.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":0,"length":9},{"style":"italic","offset":122,"length":23},{"style":"italic","offset":261,"length":9},{"style":"italic","offset":276,"length":23},{"style":"italic","offset":442,"length":9},{"style":"italic","offset":567,"length":10},{"style":"italic","offset":598,"length":9},{"style":"italic","offset":667,"length":9},{"style":"italic","offset":689,"length":9}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

steps: Microwave a vial containing 10 mL of fly food until boiling. Take care not to allow the food to boil up the sides of the vial. Stir as needed.
Allow food to cool to 55 °C . Use a probe thermometer to confirm the temperature.
Add the dissolved tetracycline to the food and mix thoroughly. The final concentration of tetracycline is approximately 0.3 mg/mL .
Allow food to cool to Room temperature . Use a probe thermometer to confirm the temperature.
Transfer Wolbachia -bearing flies onto the tetracycline-treated media.

Rear Wolbachia -free flies on regular fly food for 3 generations before using them in experiments. Tetracycline can interfere with the host's mitochondria. Providing time for the flies to recover is important to avoid confounding effects associated with mitochondrial disfunction. Ballard JWO, Melvin RG. (2007) Tetracycline treatment influences mitochondrial metabolism and mtDNA density two generations after treatment in Drosophila . Insect Mol Biol. 16(6):799–802. PMID: 18093008
After four days, clear vials of adults. (Optional) Store vials in a Drosophila incubator set to 23 °C or 25 °C .

Test progeny for Wolbachia . If the flies still have Wolbachia , continue tetracycline treatment for an additional generation and test their offspring for Wolbachia . We use the following protocol to test if flies have Wolbachia using PCR: PCR-based cytotyping to detect Wolbachia in insect tissues by J. Dylan Shropshire
Monitor vials regularly. Once adults begin emerging, transfer to a freshly prepared vial of tetracycline-treated food.
Repeat go to step #7 and go to step #8 until flies have been maintained on tetracycline-treated food for three generations.

Using an analytical balance and a pipette, add the following to a Centrifuge tubes, safe-lock, Eppendorf quality, 1.5 mL Eppendorf Catalog #022363204 : 3 mg Tetracycline hydrochloride Fisher Scientific Catalog #BP912 (CAS: 64-75-5) 50 µL diH 2 O Vortex and invert the tube until the tetracycline to completely dissolve.
